Forecast From a Successful Bear
LEAD: ONE stock that held like a bulwark through last week's selling deluge was the Growth Stock Outlook Trust, a closed-end investment fund trading on the New York Stock Exchange. Last week the shares closed at $9.50, up 7 percent. The main reason: GSO Trust's manager, Charles Allmon, met the crash with more than 75 percent of the fund's assets held in cash.

HOW VULNERABLE TO A RECESSION IS THE COUNTY?
LEAD: IF a recession develops in the wake of the stock market's collapse in mid-October, business leaders and economists say that Westchester would once again fare better than many other parts of the state or the nation. But they add that structural changes in the county's economy have stripped away some of its traditional insulation against an economic downturn, so that the effects of a decline would be more pronounced and widespread than before.

One 'Crash' WOn't Scare Away Investors
LEAD: Recent forecasts by economists about near-term levels of economic activity draw heavily on psycological and sociological presuppositions. Pushed aside are economic theories of business cycles, or the stimulative effects of the declining exchange rate. Instead economists advance two propositions; first, an economic slowdown - possibly a recession - will be caused by a ''negative wealth effect.

LEAD: Stock prices were mixed yesterday, as investors wrestled with more conflicting economic news.

Stocks Up Broadly; Dow Rises 18.90
LEAD: Stock prices moved broadly higher yesterday in continued moderate trading, and the Dow Jones industrial average was up 18.90 points, to 1,930.04. Transportation issues were particularly strong.

Coming to Terms With the Latin Debt
LEAD: As 1987 comes to a close, the only uncertainty about the Latin American debt problem is when the last of the diehard optimists will have to concede. The strategy of muddling through, cobbled in 1982 and subsequently modestly revised, has finally run its course. What 1988 holds in store is debt relief in one form or another.
